lesson:
The mirror image of お姉さん. 兄あに is "my older brother" — humble, for talking about your own family. お兄さん is the respectful version: someone else's older brother, or a direct address.

Like お姉さん, the word has drifted beyond family. お兄さん is used to address young men you don't know — a friendly, slightly casual term for any guy in roughly his 20s-30s. Shopkeepers, waitstaff, and street vendors use it. Children use it for any teenage or adult male who isn't quite おじさん territory yet.

The kanji 兄 is 口くち (mouth) on top of 儿 (legs/person). The person who speaks with authority — the elder who gives instructions. In classical Chinese, 兄 wasn't just a family term; it was a word for any elder male you respected. Japanese 兄貴あにき preserves that broader usage — it means "big bro" in yakuza and gang hierarchies, not necessarily a biological brother.

大きい is one of only two い-adjectives with an irregular prenominal form. You can say 大きい犬 (big dog) — that's fine. But you can also say 大きな犬 — same meaning, slightly softer. No other い-adjective does this. (The only other one is 小さい/小さな.) The 〜な form behaves like a な-adjective but 大きい is grammatically an い-adjective in every other way.

The kanji 大 is a person with arms and legs spread wide — taking up maximum space. It's one of the first kanji anyone learns, and it shows up in an enormous number of compounds: 大学 (university), 大人 (adult), 大好き (love), 大切 (important), 大変 (terrible/very). In every case, 大 amplifies whatever follows it.

Dictionaryおおきい
Negativeおおきくない
Pastおおきかった
Past negおおきくなかった
Adverbおおきく
て-formおおきくて

The pair to 大きい, and the only other い-adjective with a 〜な prenominal form: 小さい声 and 小さな声 both mean "a small voice." The な version tends to feel warmer, more subjective — like you're describing something with fondness rather than measuring it.

The kanji 小 is a vertical line with two smaller strokes on either side — something being divided into smaller pieces. Like 大, it anchors a huge family of compounds: 小学校 (elementary school), 小説 (novel — "small talk" that became "fiction"), 小さい itself.

Sound note: chii-sai has a drawn-out い that mimics smallness — a thin, pinched sound. Say it quietly and it practically illustrates its own meaning. Compare with oo-kii, which opens the mouth wide. Japanese adjectives sometimes sound like what they describe.

Dictionaryちいさい
Negativeちいさくない
Pastちいさかった
Past negちいさくなかった
Adverbちいさく
て-formちいさくて

Two words share this kanji. 辛い read as からい means spicy/salty/harsh. 辛い read as つらい means emotionally painful, agonizing. In writing, only context tells them apart — the kanji is identical. このカレーは辛い is clearly からい (spicy). 別れは辛い is clearly つらい (painful).

As からい, the word covers both "spicy" (chili heat) and "salty" (over-salted food). English separates these completely; Japanese doesn't. A bowl of ramen with too much salt is 辛い. A plate of mapo tofu that sets your mouth on fire is also 辛い. The common thread is intensity — flavor that's too strong, that pushes back.

The kanji 辛 originally depicted a tattooing needle — a tool that causes sharp pain. From physical pain it extended to the sharp sensation of strong flavors, and to the emotional sharpness of hardship. The needle is at the center of all three meanings.

Dictionaryからい
Negativeからくない
Pastからかった
Past negからくなかった
Adverbからく
て-formからくて

The kanji 八 looks like two lines spreading apart — an opening, an expansion. In Japanese culture, eight is a lucky number precisely because of this visual: things spreading open suggest growth and prosperity. 末広すえひろがり ("spreading toward the end") is the term for this auspicious widening shape, and 八 embodies it.

Where four is avoided (death association), eight is welcomed. Wedding gifts, business deals, and celebrations favor eights. The Shinto goddess Amaterasu rules the 八百万の神やおよろずのかみ ("eight million gods") — a phrase that really means "countless gods," using eight as a stand-in for infinity.

Pronunciation quirk: 八 before certain sounds becomes はっ: 八百はっぴゃく, 八千はっせん, 八分はっぷん. The っ is the tongue getting ready for the next consonant.

The あ member of the place trio: ここ (here, near me), そこ (there, near you), あそこ (over there, away from both of us). あそこ is the odd one out in form — ここ and そこ are two syllables, but あそこ is three. The extra そ just developed historically; there's no deep reason for it.

あそこに見える建物 (the building visible over there). あそこのラーメン屋、うまいよ (that ramen place over there is good). The word points at something you can both see but neither of you is near. It requires shared visual reference — you wouldn't use あそこ on the phone unless both speakers know the location.

In casual speech, あそこ sometimes gets shortened to あっこ in some dialects, or replaced entirely by あっち (that direction). The polite form is あちら.

One of only two irregular verbs in Japanese (with する). Every other verb follows the ichidan or godan pattern without exception. 来る conjugates like nothing else: 来こない, 来きます, 来きた, 来きて. The reading of 来 itself changes — く, こ, き — depending on the form. No pattern predicts this. Memorize the forms directly.

Remember the sound pair from 行く's lesson? いく pushes outward (going). くる pulls inward (coming). The mouth mirrors the meaning: く starts closed and rounds in. Things come to you.

来る is also the helper verb in the 〜てくる pattern, which indicates movement toward the speaker or a change that has been developing up to now: 雨が降ってきた (it started raining — the rain came). 疲れてきた (I'm getting tired — fatigue is arriving).

Particles: 来る takes に or へ for the destination, and から for the origin. 日本に来る (come to Japan). アメリカから来た (came from America).

Passive に来られる: someone's coming affects you — often negatively. 急に来られて困った (they showed up suddenly and it was a problem). に marks the person who came.

Causative を／に来させる: making or letting someone come. を for coercive — 部下を来させる (make the subordinate come). に for permissive.

Irregular自動詞
Dictionaryくるに／へ
Negativeこないに／へ
Politeきますに／へ
Polite negきませんに／へ
Pastきたに／へ
Polite pastきましたに／へ
て-formきてに／へ
Volitionalこように／へ
Polite volきましょうに／へ
Passiveこられるに
Causativeこさせるを／に
Caus-passこさせられるに
Potentialこられるに／へ
Imperativeこいに／へ
Imp なさいきなさいに／へ
Cond ～ばくればに／へ
Cond ～たらきたらに／へ

前 does double duty across space and time, but in opposite directions from what you'd expect. Spatially, 前 means "in front" — what's ahead of you. Temporally, it means "before" — what came earlier. 駅の前 (in front of the station). 三年前 (three years ago). Front and past — the same word.

This isn't as contradictory as it seems. In Japanese (and Chinese) conceptual metaphor, the past is in front of you because you can see it — it already happened, it's known. The future is behind you because you can't see what's coming. This is the opposite of the English metaphor where we "look forward" to the future and "leave the past behind." 前 carries the Japanese orientation: what you face is what you know.

The kanji 前 shows a foot (止) and a knife (刂) under a cover — the original meaning was to cut something and present it, to bring it forward. From "bringing forward" came "in front of" and "previously."

meaning: five days, fifth of the month
lesson:
Itsuka — from 五いつつ compressed with 日か (day). The same native-number-plus-か pattern as 六日 (むいか), 七日 (なのか), and the rest of the first ten days.

Be careful: いつか (five days / the fifth) sounds exactly like いつか meaning "someday." The two are completely unrelated — one comes from the number five, the other from the question word いつ + the indefinite か. Context handles the ambiguity: 五日に会おう (let's meet on the fifth) vs. いつか会おう (let's meet someday). In writing, the kanji 五日 vs. hiragana いつか keeps them apart.

meaning: full, a lot, as much as possible
lesson:
Written in kanji as 一杯 — "one cup." How does "one cup" become "a lot"? Because in drinking culture, 一杯 started as "one cup (of sake)" and evolved through euphemism. 一杯飲もう (let's have a drink) never means literally one cup. It means let's drink — possibly a lot. From there, いっぱい drifted into a general intensifier meaning "full" or "lots of."

お腹がいっぱい (I'm full — stomach is stuffed). 人がいっぱい (there are tons of people). いっぱい食べてね (eat lots, OK?). It's casual and warm — the kind of word grandmothers use. For formal contexts, たくさん is the safer choice.

The counter use survives too: コーヒー一杯 (one cup of coffee). Here, 杯 is the counter for cups/glasses, and it genuinely means one. Context makes the difference between "one cup" and "a ton."

Nine has two readings: きゅう (Sino-Japanese) and く (also Sino-Japanese, but shorter). Like four, nine has a death problem: く sounds like 苦く (suffering). Hospital rooms avoid 4 and 9. Floors skip them. Some apartment buildings go from 8 to 10.

When counting with counters, きゅう is the standard: 九時くじ (nine o'clock) uses く, but 九月くがつ (September) also uses く. The pattern is inconsistent — some compounds fossilized with く, others with きゅう. 九十きゅうじゅう (ninety) uses きゅう. You learn each compound's reading individually.

The kanji 九 was originally a picture of a bent arm — the elbow. Why an arm for nine? The theory is that when counting on fingers, after using all ten, you bend back to the elbow for nine (counting down). It's uncertain, but it's a better story than no story.

Say it out loud: sup-pai. That すっ sound — the sucking-in, pursing feeling — is exactly what your face does when you bite into something sour. Few Japanese words wear their meaning in their mouth-feel as clearly as this one.

The kanji 酸 has the sake radical (酉) on the left — the same component in 酒 (alcohol) and 醤油 (soy sauce). Fermentation is the connection: things that ferment can go sour. 酸 also means "acid" in chemistry (酸素 is oxygen, 酸性 is acidity), connecting sourness to its chemical basis.

Japan's five basic tastes each have their own word: 甘い (sweet), 辛い (spicy/salty), 苦い (にがい, bitter), 酸っぱい (sour), and 旨い/うまい (umami/savory). Japan's contribution to global food science was recognizing umami as a distinct fifth taste — a discovery made in 1908 by chemist Ikeda Kikunae, who isolated glutamate from kelp broth.

Dictionaryすっぱい
Negativeすっぱくない
Pastすっぱかった
Past negすっぱくなかった
Adverbすっぱく
て-formすっぱくて

違う covers two English concepts that feel very different: "to be different" and "to be wrong." これは違う can mean "this is different" or "this is wrong" depending on context. The logic: if something deviates from what's expected or correct, it 違う. Deviation is the core meaning — whether that deviation is neutral (mere difference) or negative (error) depends on the situation.

In conversation, 違う！ is a sharp correction — "Wrong!" or "No, that's not it!" It's direct enough that some people soften it: ちょっと違うかな (it's a bit off, maybe). But as a standalone interjection, 違う can sound blunt.

The adjective form 違ちがい (difference) shows up in the compound 間違まちがい (mistake — a "gap-difference"). And 勘違かんちがい (misunderstanding) is literally "an intuition that's off."

Particles: 違う takes と to mark what something differs from — 予想と違う (different from what I expected). Or it stands alone with が as the subject marker: 答えが違う (the answer is wrong).

Passive: not commonly used — differing isn't something done to someone.

Causative: extremely rare with this verb.

細い is thin in the lateral sense — narrow, slender, fine. Not thin like paper (that's 薄い, うすい — thin in depth). 細い is a thin line, a thin road, thin fingers, a thin voice. The axis of thinness matters in Japanese: 細い compresses width; 薄い compresses thickness.

道が細い (the road is narrow). 細い糸 (a thin thread). 声が細い (a thin, weak voice). In that last usage, 細い implies fragility — something easily broken or lost.

The kanji 細 combines 糸いと (thread) with 田た (field). A thread through a field — fine, delicate, detailed. It also appears in 細こまかい (detailed/fine-grained), shifting from physical thinness to conceptual precision.

Dictionaryほそい
Negativeほそくない
Pastほそかった
Past negほそくなかった
Adverbほそく
て-formほそくて

The native Japanese counter continues: ひとつ, ふたつ, みっつ. The root み is the original Japanese word for three, surviving in 三日みっか (the third), 三人みたり in archaic Japanese, now さんにん, and of course 三つ.

The double っ in みっつ is a feature of the native counting system — ひとつ, ふたつ, みっつ, よっつ, いつつ, むっつ, ななつ, やっつ, ここのつ, とお. Notice the っ appears in 3, 4, 6, and 8. These aren't random — they're the result of historical sound changes where syllables compressed over centuries of rapid speech.

meaning: eight days, eighth of the month
lesson:
Youka — from 八やっつ + 日か, compressed beyond recognition. The や of やっつ shifted to よう, the same way old pronunciations drift when words are spoken fast enough for long enough. You can still hear the ghost of や in よう if you squint with your ears.

A useful memory trick for the first ten day-readings: they all end in か (from 日), and the number part comes from the native counting series (ひ、ふ、み、よ、い、む、な、や、ここの、とお). Learn those ten roots and the date readings become recoverable — even if the compression makes some of them hard to spot at first.

高校こうこう (high school) + 生せい (student/person born into). Japanese has a clean system for student labels: 小学生 (elementary), 中学生 (middle school), 高校生 (high school), 大学生 (university). Swap the school name, keep 生.

高校生 occupies a specific cultural space in Japan. It's the last stage of mandatory-track education (technically 高校 isn't mandatory, but 99% of students attend). High school uniforms — sailor suits for girls, gakuran for boys, or modern blazers — are iconic. Anime and manga are obsessed with this age range for a reason: it's the last period of structured community before adult life scatters everyone.

The word 高校 itself is an abbreviation of 高等学校こうとうがっこう ("higher-level school"). Almost nobody uses the full form in daily speech.

上手 literally means "upper hand" — the hand that's on top, the position of advantage and skill. Its opposite is 下手へた (unskilled — "lower hand"). The pair is fundamental and shows up constantly.

日本語が上手ですね is the compliment every foreigner in Japan hears, regardless of actual ability. It's standard politeness — acknowledge the effort, praise the attempt. How you respond matters: a flat ありがとうございます can work, but いえいえ、まだまだです (no no, I still have far to go) is the culturally expected deflection.

Note: 上手 is typically used about others, not yourself. Calling yourself 上手 sounds boastful. For self-assessment, Japanese uses 得意とくい ("one's strength") instead: 料理が得意です (cooking is my strong suit). The distinction is social, not grammatical — 上手 praises, 得意 describes.

Dictionaryじょうず
Negativeじょうずじゃない
Pastじょうずだった
Past negじょうずじゃなかった
Adverbじょうずに
て-formじょうずで

The kanji 強 has a bug (虫) component and a bow (弓) component. One interpretation: a beetle's hard shell — the toughness of an insect that resists being crushed. Beetles are strong relative to their size, and the character captures that resilient, push-back kind of strength.

強い covers physical strength (彼は強い (he's strong)), potency (このコーヒーは強い (this coffee is strong)), resilience (風に強い (resistant to wind)), and competitive dominance (将棋が強い (good at shogi)). It's used for anything that doesn't yield easily.

Its opposite 弱よわい (weak) is the expected pair. But watch for 強い in compound verbs: 強引ごういん (forceful/pushy), 強制きょうせい (compulsion), 勉強べんきょう (study — literally "forced effort"). That last one reframes studying as an act of strength: pushing yourself to learn even when it's hard.

Dictionaryつよい
Negativeつよくない
Pastつよかった
Past negつよくなかった
Adverbつよく
て-formつよくて

This is the last and strangest of the irregular day-of-month readings. 二十日 is read はつか — not にじゅうにち, not anything you'd predict from the kanji. The reading comes from the old native Japanese counting system: はた meant "twenty" in ancient Japanese (a word that's otherwise extinct), and か is the native counter for days. はたか smoothed into はつか over time.

By this point you've seen the full set of irregular day readings: ついたち (1st), ふつか (2nd), みっか (3rd), よっか (4th), いつか (5th), むいか (6th), なのか (7th), ようか (8th), ここのか (9th), とおか (10th), and now はつか (20th). After the 10th, days 11–19 and 21–31 all use the regular Sino-Japanese numbers + にち. The 20th is the one last holdout of the old system — a fossil floating alone in the middle of the month.

The kanji 左 shows a hand (ナ, originally a hand shape) reaching toward a carpenter's square (工). The right hand (右) reaches toward a mouth (口) — the eating hand. The left hand reaches toward a tool — the helping hand. This distinction between the tool-hand and the mouth-hand is baked into the characters themselves.

You already know 右みぎ (right) from an earlier lesson. The pair works the same way in compounds: 左側ひだりがわ (left side), 左手ひだりて (left hand). When giving directions, Japanese uses 左に曲がる (turn left) — the に marks the direction of the turn.

The on'yomi さ shows up in one compound worth knowing: 左右さゆう (left and right — also means "to influence" or "to control"). The idea: if you can move something left and right, you control it.

meaning: two days, second of the month
lesson:
ふつか — and pay attention, because this reading is doing something interesting. The native Japanese word for two is ふた. Add the day counter か and the た hardens into a glottal つ: ふたか → ふつか. The same consonant hardening happens in みっか (3rd) and よっか (4th). It's not random — Japanese smooths these out the same way every time.

二日酔ふつかよい (hangover) — literally "two-day drunk." The word captures the misery perfectly: you drank one day and you're still feeling it the next. This is probably the compound where you'll encounter ふつか most often in real Japanese, especially on Monday mornings.

よっつ — four of something, anything. The native つ-counter is the universal fallback when you don't know the right counter word or can't be bothered. Ordering four dumplings? よっつください. Nobody will blink.

The word 四つ角よつかど (intersection) is worth knowing — a "four-corner" is what Japanese calls a crossroads. And 四つん這よつんばい (on all fours) literally means "four-way crawl." The native よ feels physical and concrete compared to the Sino-Japanese し, which lives in compounds and formality. Four people is よにん, not しにん — because しにん is indistinguishable from 死人 (dead person), and nobody wants to count their friends that way.

Japanese has two "warm" words and the distinction matters. 暖かい is warm air, warm weather, warm rooms — warmth that surrounds you. 温かい (also read あたたかい) is warm food, warm water, warm feelings — warmth you touch or warmth that's metaphorical. Same pronunciation, different kanji, different domains. 暖かい日 (a warm day) uses 暖. 温かいスープ (warm soup) uses 温. In practice, hiragana あたたかい sidesteps the choice entirely, and many native speakers don't stress the distinction in casual writing.

The kanji 暖 has 日 (sun) on the left — warmth from the sun, warmth in the atmosphere. Compare 温, which has 氵 (water radical) — warmth in liquids, warmth you feel with your body.

Sound anchor: a-ta-ta-ka-i has that repeated た syllable that almost sounds like teeth chattering before the warmth kicks in. Five syllables for one adjective — it's long, and that length gives it a cozy, unhurried feeling when spoken.

Dictionaryあたたかい
Negativeあたたかくない
Pastあたたかかった
Past negあたたかくなかった
Adverbあたたかく
て-formあたたかくて

Japanese splits existence in two. いる is for things that move on their own — people, animals, insects. ある is for everything else — objects, places, ideas, events. A book is on the table? 本がある. A cat is on the table? 猫がいる. Get this wrong and you'll sound like you think the book is alive or the cat is furniture.

Irregular negative: ある's negative is ない, not あらない. This is the only godan verb that does this. And ない is so fundamental to the language that it barely feels like a conjugation — it's practically its own word. 時間がない (there's no time). お金がない (there's no money). You'll use ない a hundred times before you consciously connect it back to ある.

ある also means "to have" in the sense of possession: 車がある (I have a car). Notice the particle — が, not を. The car isn't something you're acting on. It just exists in your life. This is different from 持っている (physically holding/carrying), which uses を.

Particles: ある takes が to mark what exists — 問題がある (there's a problem). Use に for the location: 机の上に本がある (there's a book on the desk). The thing exists (が), at a place (に).

Passive/Causative: ある doesn't have standard passive or causative forms — existence isn't something you do to someone or make someone do. The verb just states that something is there.

五段 Godan自動詞

Negativeないが
Politeありますが
Polite negありませんが
Pastあったが
Polite pastありましたが
て-formあってが
Volitional

Passive
Causative

Imperative
Imp なさい
Cond ～ばあればが
Cond ～たらあったらが

いい is one of the first words every learner picks up — and then immediately runs into a wall. It's irregular. The moment you try to conjugate it, いい vanishes and よい takes over. Negative? よくない. Past? よかった. て-form? よくて. Only the dictionary form is いい. Everything else reverts to よい, which is the original, formal form of the word.

Historically, よい came first. いい is a casual contraction that took over in spoken Japanese — but the conjugation system never updated to match. So you get this split personality: いい for the base form in conversation, よい for everything else. It's the same word in two outfits.

いい covers a huge range in daily conversation. いいよ can mean "sure, go ahead," "that's fine," "no thanks" (declining politely), or "it's good" — all depending on tone and context. もういい can mean "that's enough" or "I'm done" — sometimes with frustration, sometimes with satisfaction. It's a Swiss army knife word where intonation does most of the work.

Dictionaryいい / よい
Negativeよくない
Pastよかった
Past negよくなかった
Adverbよく
て-formよくて

上 is one of the most overloaded kanji in the language. As うえ it means physical "above" — 机の上 (on the desk), 上を見る (look up). But the readings multiply: うえ、うわ、かみ、あ(がる)、のぼ(る)、じょう. Each reading unlocks different meanings.

The one you need right now is the positional noun: 〜の上. In Japanese, "on the desk" isn't a preposition like English — it's a noun phrase. 机 (desk) + の (possessive) + 上 (top/above) = "the desk's above" = on the desk. This noun-based location system is how Japanese handles all spatial relationships: 上、下、中、前、後ろ、横、隣 are all nouns, not prepositions.

上 also means "superior" in rank or quality: 年上としうえ (older/senior), 目上めうえ (a social superior). In a culture built on vertical relationships, the metaphor of above = higher status is wired deep into the vocabulary.

The kanji 駅 has 馬うま (horse) on the left. Before trains existed, 駅 meant a relay station on a highway — a place where you changed horses. The post-horse system was how messages and travelers moved across Japan for centuries. When railroads arrived in the Meiji era, the word transferred seamlessly: a station is still where you stop, switch, and continue your journey. The horse is gone but the character remembers.

In modern Japan, 駅 means specifically a train station. Bus stops are バス停. Airports are 空港. 駅 belongs to rail. And because trains are the circulatory system of Japanese daily life, 駅 compounds are everywhere: 駅前えきまえ (the area in front of a station — often the commercial heart of a neighborhood), 駅弁えきべん (a boxed lunch sold at train stations — a whole culinary tradition).

Written in kanji as 美味しい — "beautiful taste" — but almost always written in hiragana: おいしい. The kanji spelling exists but feels literary and stiff for such an everyday word. You'll see おいしい in hiragana everywhere.

The word traces back to the honorific prefix お + いしい, which itself likely comes from an old word meaning "good." That お wasn't always polite — it started as a genuine honorific, making the word originally "nobly delicious." Over time the politeness bleached out and it became the standard neutral word for "tasty."

おいしい vs うまい: Both mean "delicious" but they live in different social spaces. おいしい is neutral and polite — anyone can use it anywhere. うまい is rougher, more visceral, more masculine — the word you'd use after biting into something and feeling genuine pleasure. Women and children tend to default to おいしい. Men in casual settings often reach for うまい. Both are compliments, but うまい feels more like a gut reaction and おいしい more like an evaluation.

Dictionaryおいしい
Negativeおいしくない
Pastおいしかった
Past negおいしくなかった
Adverbおいしく
て-formおいしくて

The kanji say 昨日 — "previous day." The reading says きのう. These two have nothing to do with each other. きのう is a native Japanese word (from き, related to an old word for "past," + のう, a variant of の日). The kanji 昨日 is a Chinese compound. They got married by convention — the Japanese word got the Chinese kanji bolted on as a label. This is called jukujikun, and you've seen it before with 今日きょう and 明日あした.

There's a more formal reading: さくじつ — the straight Sino-Japanese pronunciation of the same characters. You'll hear さくじつ in news broadcasts and formal writing. きのう is everyday conversation. Same kanji, two different registers, two different readings.

きれい is a trap. It ends in い, so every instinct says it's an い-adjective. It's not. It's a な-adjective. きれいい人 is wrong. きれいな人 is correct. This catches essentially every beginner, and it's worth burning into memory now: きれい takes な, not い.

The word does double duty. きれいな人 means a beautiful person. きれいな部屋 means a clean room. Beautiful and clean — two meanings that feel unrelated in English but share a root idea in Japanese: the absence of mess, disorder, impurity. Something きれい has been cleared of anything that shouldn't be there.

The kanji 綺麗 are so complex that even native Japanese speakers rarely write them by hand. 綺 alone has 14 strokes. In practice, きれい in hiragana is the norm everywhere except printed text. Don't bother learning to write the kanji — just recognize them when you see them.

Dictionaryきれい
Negativeきれいじゃない
Pastきれいだった
Past negきれいじゃなかった
Adverbきれいに
て-formきれいで

五 is the number of fingers on a hand, and the earliest versions of this character might actually represent that — an X sandwiched between two lines, possibly showing crossed fingers or a counting gesture. But the form is abstract enough that scholars still argue about it.

What's more useful: ご is the workhorse reading. 五分 (ごふん, five minutes), 五月 (ごがつ, May). The native reading いつ survives only in 五つ and 五日. And the on'yomi has a quirk: ご is voiced (the G vibrates), so when it sits before certain consonants, things get messy. 五本 (ごほん) keeps the ほ, but 五階 (ごかい) keeps the か. Compare with 三, which triggers consonant changes all over the place (三本 = さんぼん). 五 is relatively well-behaved.

Four syllables — ko-ko-no-tsu — making it the longest in the native counting series. While ひとつ through やっつ clip along briskly, ここのつ takes its time. When you're ordering nine of something at a shop, you can almost feel the shopkeeper counting along with the syllables.

The root ここの is one of the oldest Japanese number words, predating any Chinese influence. Linguists note that the native numbers one through ten show no clear pattern or mathematical relationship to each other — they're pure arbitrary labels, each its own ancient word. The Sino-Japanese numbers (いち、に、さん…) arrived later as a systematic import. The native series is a relic of prehistoric Japanese, and ここのつ, with its drawn-out sound, feels the most archaic of the set.

Break it apart: お (honorific prefix) + 願い (wish, desire). 願う (ねがう) means "to wish for, to pray for" — the kind of earnest hoping you do at a shrine. Adding お elevates it: お願い is a wish wrapped in politeness.

In practice, お願いします is one of the most-used phrases in Japanese. It means roughly "please" but with more weight — you're asking someone to do something for you and acknowledging the favor. Ordering at a restaurant: これ、お願いします. Asking for help: お願いします. Ending a self-introduction: よろしくお願いします — literally "I wish for your favorable treatment." The phrase is social lubricant, used dozens of times a day.

The casual version drops the します: just お願い！ — a direct, slightly childlike "please!" Kids use it constantly. Adults use it when the formality of します would be stiff among friends.

The kanji 答 is 竹 (bamboo) over 合 (to fit, match). The bamboo part is thought to reference the bamboo slips that Chinese officials wrote responses on. An answer is something that fits — it matches the question like a lid fits a container.

Surprising grammar: 答える is technically intransitive in Japanese. You don't answer something (を) — you answer to something (に). 質問に答える (answer a question — literally "respond to a question"). The question isn't a direct object you act on; it's a target you respond toward. This feels unnatural to English speakers but it's consistent with how Japanese treats communication verbs.

答える vs 返事する: 答える is giving a substantive answer — responding to a question with content. 返事する (へんじする) is simply responding — acknowledging that someone called your name, replying to a message, saying something back. A teacher asks a math question: you 答える. Your mother calls your name: you 返事する.

Particles: 答える takes に — the question or prompt you're responding to. 質問に答える (answer a question). The に marks what you're directing your response toward.

Passive に答えられる: rare but possible. そう答えられると困る (if I'm answered that way, I'm stuck). に marks who gave the answer.

Causative を／に答えさせる: making someone answer. 生徒に答えさせる (make the student answer). に marks the person forced to respond.

一段 Ichidan自動詞

Politeこたえますに
Polite negこたえませんに
Pastこたえたに
Polite pastこたえましたに
て-formこたえてに
Volitionalこたえように
Polite volこたえましょうに
Passiveこたえられるに
Causativeこたえさせるに
Caus-passこたえさせられるに
Potentialこたえられるに
Imperativeこたえろに
Imp なさいこたえなさいに
Cond ～ばこたえればに
Cond ～たらこたえたらに

先 points forward in space and time simultaneously. The kanji is a picture of a foot (儿) beneath something extending outward (⺧) — a foot stepping ahead. That "out ahead" meaning branches in every direction.

Spatial: 先に行く (go ahead, go first). この先 (up ahead, beyond this point). 指先 (ゆびさき, fingertip — the "ahead" of a finger). Temporal: 先に食べて (eat first, before me). 先のこと (things in the future).

But here's the twist — 先 can also mean "before" in the past sense. 先週 (せんしゅう, last week). 先月 (せんげつ, last month). The logic: what went ahead of you in time has already passed. The same word means both "previously" and "in the future" depending entirely on context. When you say 先に帰った (went home first/ahead), it's past. When you say 先が見えない (can't see what's ahead), it's future. Context always resolves it.

Japanese has a cluster of "cold" words and mixing them up sounds strange. 寒い is cold you feel in your body from the environment — cold weather, a cold room, the cold of winter. 冷たい (つめたい) is cold you touch — cold water, cold hands, a cold drink. 寒い is about air temperature. 冷たい is about surface temperature.

The kanji 寒 is elaborate: a roof (宀) over a person surrounded by ice crystals and grass. The whole character evokes huddling inside while it's freezing outside. In oracle bone script, the earliest versions showed a person packed in straw insulation inside a house — the ancient Chinese equivalent of wrapping up in blankets.

Slang use: 寒い also means "lame" or "cringey" in casual speech — a bad joke is 寒い. The metaphor: it makes the room go cold. そのギャグ、寒い… (that joke is cold/cringe…). This slang is common enough that you'll hear it regularly.

Dictionaryさむい
Negativeさむくない
Pastさむかった
Past negさむくなかった
Adverbさむく
て-formさむくて

You already know 四 as よん — the native Japanese reading. This entry is for the Sino-Japanese reading: し. And し has a problem. It sounds exactly like 死 (し, death).

This isn't superstition — it's a real social force. Many hospitals in Japan skip room 4 and floor 4. Apartment buildings sometimes go from 3 to 5. Phone numbers and license plates with し are avoided. In everyday counting, よん has largely replaced し because people actively dodge the death-sound. You'll hear よんにん (four people) far more often than しにん — and しにん (死人, dead person) is the exact reason why.

Where し survives: 四月 (しがつ, April), 四時 (よじ, 4 o'clock — but note: this one uses よ, not し), and some fixed compounds. The pattern: when the word is old and established enough, し sticks around. In newer or flexible contexts, よん wins.

meaning: three days, third of the month
lesson:
三日坊主 (みっかぼうず) — "three-day monk." Someone who shaves their head, enters a monastery, then quits after three days. It's the Japanese idiom for people who can't stick with anything. Start a diet? Three-day monk. New Year's resolution? Three-day monk. The phrase is used constantly and it's the most natural way to encounter みっか in the wild.

The reading: み (three) + か (day counter), with the consonant doubling (っ) that Japanese inserts for flow. Same mechanism as よっか (4th) and いっか — wait, いっか doesn't exist. The first of the month refused to follow the pattern and kept its own ancient reading (ついたち) instead. The third of the month, at least, plays by the rules.

The mirror of 上. Where 上 points up, 下 points down — and it's equally overloaded with readings and meanings. As した: under, below, beneath. 机の下 (under the desk). 下を向く (look down).

The kanji is a horizontal line with a stroke pointing downward — as simple as it gets. One of the first kanji anyone learns, alongside 上 and 中.

Like 上, the social dimension matters. 下 in compounds often means inferior, subordinate, or lower in hierarchy: 部下 (ぶか, subordinate), 年下 (としした, younger/junior), 下手 (へた, unskilled — literally "lower hand"). The vertical metaphor runs through the whole language: good things go up, bad things go down, and social position maps directly onto spatial position.

Other readings you'll encounter: した、しも、もと、さ(がる)、くだ(さる)、お(りる)、か、げ. Each unlocks different words. 下 might be the kanji with the most distinct readings in common use.

Three kanji: 大 (big) + 丈 (stature, measure) + 夫 (man). A big, sturdy man. The original Chinese meaning was something like "a man of great stature" — reliable, solid, someone you can depend on. From "a dependable person" to "it's dependable" to "it's fine" to "I'm OK" — the meaning drifted from describing a person to describing a situation.

大丈夫 is one of the most versatile words in daily Japanese. 大丈夫ですか？ can mean "Are you OK?" (after someone stumbles), "Is it all right?" (asking permission), or "Will it work?" (checking feasibility). 大丈夫です as an answer means "I'm fine," "It's OK," or — increasingly common in modern Japanese — "No thank you." That last one confuses even some native speakers: 袋、大丈夫です at a convenience store means "I don't need a bag" even though the words literally say "a bag is fine."

The "no thank you" usage is recent enough that older speakers sometimes find it jarring. But it's everywhere now, especially among younger people. The word has become a polite softener for declining things — because saying いらない (don't need it) feels too blunt.

Dictionaryだいじょうぶ
Negativeだいじょうぶじゃない
Pastだいじょうぶだった
Past negだいじょうぶじゃなかった
Adverbだいじょうぶに
て-formだいじょうぶで

大人 is another jukujikun — the kanji say "big person" (大 + 人) but the reading おとな is a native Japanese word unrelated to either character's individual pronunciation. おとな likely derives from old Japanese words for "mature" or "calm" — the sense that an adult is someone who has settled down.

The word carries more cultural weight than English "adult." 大人になる (become an adult) isn't just about turning 18 or 20 — it implies acquiring social grace, self-control, and the ability to read situations. When someone says 大人の対応 (an adult response), they mean a composed, mature reaction — not rising to provocation, not making a scene. 大人しい (おとなしい, quiet and well-behaved) literally means "adult-like."

The opposite, 子供 (こども), doesn't just mean "child" — calling someone's behavior 子供っぽい (childish) is a real insult. The 大人/子供 axis is a moral spectrum, not just an age bracket.

The transitive partner of 出る (でる, to come out). Where 出る describes something emerging on its own, 出す means you make it come out. ゴミを出す (take out the trash). 手紙を出す (send a letter — put it out into the postal system). 宿題を出す (submit homework — from a student's perspective, or assign homework — from a teacher's). The same verb, different agents, different meanings.

〜出す as a suffix: Attach 出す to another verb's stem and it means "to burst into doing" — a sudden, often uncontrollable start. 泣き出す (burst into tears). 走り出す (break into a run). 笑い出す (burst out laughing). The action erupts out of you like something escaping. This pattern is extremely common and adds a vivid "suddenly" nuance that's hard to express in English without extra words.

Particles: 出す takes を — the thing you put out is your direct object. お金を出す (put out money — pay). 声を出す (let out your voice — speak up).

Potential が出せる: が — what can be put out becomes the subject. 大きい声が出せない (I can't raise my voice).

Passive に出される: に marks who did it. 先生に宿題を出された (the teacher assigned us homework — and I'm affected by it).

Causative に…を出させる: に marks the person, を marks the thing. 子供にゴミを出させる (make the kid take out the trash).

五段 Godan他動詞 — pair: 出る (でる)

ちち is how you refer to your own father when speaking to outsiders. It's the humble, in-group term — used when the social context demands you lower your own family. 父は会社員です (My father is a company employee — said to a teacher, a colleague, anyone outside your family).

The counterpart you already know: お父さん (おとうさん) — the warm, respectful form. You call your own father お父さん to his face, but when talking about him to outsiders, you switch to ちち. And when referring to someone else's father, you use お父さん (or the more formal お父様). This in-group/out-group switching system applies to all family terms: 母/お母さん, 兄/お兄さん, 姉/お姉さん.

The kanji 父 in its earliest form showed a hand holding a stone axe — the authority figure, the provider. It's one of the oldest and simplest family kanji.

Same system as 父. はは is your mother when you're talking about her to the outside world. お母さん is what you call her at home or how you refer to someone else's mother. Mix them up and you either sound like you're bragging about your own family (using お母さん to outsiders) or disrespecting someone else's (using はは for their mother).

The kanji 母 in oracle bone script was a woman figure with two dots at the chest — a nursing mother. Those two dots survive in the modern character as the two strokes inside the frame. It's a direct, physical representation of motherhood that has stayed remarkably stable over three thousand years of writing evolution.

Sound: ha-ha — the same doubled consonant that English "mama" and "papa" use. Infant babbling produces these sounds universally, which is why words for parents sound similar across unrelated languages. はは, mama, ama, mutter — they all started in a baby's mouth.

The kanji 月 was a crescent moon in oracle bone script — a curved shape with a line or two inside suggesting the surface markings. Over millennia of stylistic evolution, the crescent straightened into a rectangle, but the meaning never changed. Every time you see 月 in a compound, either the moon or the month is nearby.

Because the moon marks months. 月 read as つき means the physical moon. Read as がつ or げつ, it means "month": 一月 (いちがつ, January), 月曜日 (げつようび, Monday — "moon day"). The dual meaning isn't coincidence — the lunar calendar defined months in Japan until the Meiji era adopted the Gregorian calendar in 1873.

月が綺麗ですね — "The moon is beautiful, isn't it?" This sentence is famous in Japanese culture as a subtle way of saying "I love you," attributed (possibly apocryphally) to the novelist Natsume Soseki. He supposedly said this was how a Japanese person would translate "I love you" — not with a direct declaration, but by sharing a moment of beauty. Whether the story is true or not, it's become a cultural reference that every Japanese person knows.

The kanji 妹 is 女 (woman) + 未 (not yet). A woman who is "not yet" — not yet grown, not yet mature. The younger sibling captured in a single character.

Like 父 and 母, 妹 is the in-group term for your own younger sister. There's no standard お妹さん equivalent — for someone else's younger sister, you'd say 妹さん (いもうとさん). The family-term hierarchy in Japanese: older siblings get full honorifics (お兄さん、お姉さん), younger siblings get a simple さん at most. Seniority matters even in the vocabulary.

The いもうと reading comes from ancient Japanese いも (a word for a beloved woman or sister) + と (person). いも appears in classical poetry as a term of endearment, but in modern Japanese it only survives inside words like いもうと.

Touch-cold, not weather-cold. 冷たい is for things your skin contacts: 冷たい水 (cold water), 手が冷たい (my hands are cold), 冷たいビール (a cold beer). The weather being cold? That's 寒い. A cold room? Also 寒い, because you feel it in the air. But reach into a refrigerator and the shelf is 冷たい.

The kanji 冷 has the ice radical (冫) on the left — two dots representing ice crystals. The right side 令 provides the sound (れい) and was originally a person kneeling to receive orders, though that meaning isn't relevant here. The ice radical is your anchor: 冷 = cold (physical).

Metaphorical extension: 冷たい人 means a cold-hearted person — someone emotionally distant, indifferent. 彼女は冷たい (she's cold — emotionally). The jump from physical cold to emotional coldness works the same way in Japanese and English. A 冷たい態度 (cold attitude) sends the same shiver.

Dictionaryつめたい
Negativeつめたくない
Pastつめたかった
Past negつめたくなかった
Adverbつめたく
て-formつめたくて

The kanji 弟 originally depicted a spool wound with thread, with the thread wrapping downward — the idea of sequence, of what comes after. A younger brother is the one who comes after in the birth order. The same character appears in 兄弟 (きょうだい, siblings) where 兄 (older brother) pairs with 弟 (younger brother) to mean siblings in general.

おとうと follows the same in-group/out-group rules as all family terms. For someone else's younger brother: 弟さん (おとうとさん). For your own: just 弟.

The on'yomi てい／だい shows up in 弟子 (でし, disciple/apprentice — literally "younger-brother child"). The master-disciple relationship in Japanese tradition mirrors the older-younger sibling dynamic: the disciple owes respect, obedience, and loyalty to the master, just as a younger sibling does to an elder.
